headline: The Architecture of Ontological Design Systems
alternativeHeadline: From utility classes to semantic intent — a three-tier approach to CSS architecture
description: A deep exploration of the three-tier architecture behind the Genesis Semantic Design System: content semantics in HTML, role mapping in the interface layer, and visual implementation in the engine layer.

about.name: Three-Tier Architecture
about.description: The Genesis system separates concerns into three tiers: Tier 1 (Content) defines what the data is in HTML with semantic class names. Tier 2 (Interface) maps content roles to ontological categories using SCSS mixins. Tier 3 (Engine) produces the actual CSS properties.

about.name: Visual Design Element Ownership
about.description: Each CSS property belongs to exactly one ontological category. Padding belongs to entity. Font-size belongs to cognition. Grid belongs to environment. Box-shadow belongs to atmosphere. This prevents property conflicts across concerns.

about.name: Hierarchy Level Rules
about.description: Every HTML element falls into one of four hierarchy levels: Page Layout (environment + atmosphere), Section (environment), Component (entity), and Leaf Element (cognition or synapse). Each level has required and forbidden mixin categories.

articleBody:
The utility-class revolution solved one problem — it eliminated the cascade — but introduced another. Class names became visual descriptions rather than semantic ones. A div wearing 'p-4 bg-gray-800 rounded-lg shadow-md' communicates nothing about what it represents.

The Genesis Design System takes a different approach. Its three-tier architecture separates concerns completely. Tier 1 is content: pure HTML with semantic class names like .research-paper or .product-card. Tier 2 is the interface: SCSS mixins that map semantic roles to ontological categories. Tier 3 is the engine: the only place where raw CSS properties exist.

This means subdomain developers never write CSS properties. They describe what their content is, and the theme engine determines how it looks. Change the engine, and every subdomain updates automatically.

The system enforces a strict hierarchy. Page-level wrappers use environment and atmosphere. Sections use environment. Components use entity. Leaf elements like headings and buttons use cognition or synapse. Each level has forbidden categories — you cannot apply entity to a layout wrapper or atmosphere to a heading.

Visual properties are owned by exactly one category. Padding belongs to entity. Font properties belong to cognition. Grid and flex belong to environment. Box-shadow belongs to atmosphere. Hover and focus states belong to synapse. Animation belongs to state.

This ownership model prevents property conflicts. When two mixins affect the same element, they never compete for the same CSS properties. Refactoring one layer cannot break another.

The architecture enables a living genome: new variants are proposed, reviewed by AI agents, and documented with their origin story. The system grows through use, not through top-down mandates.
